data.title: A Win-Win Exit Strategy For Trump On Tariffs
data.description:
On April 8, 2025, the Wall Street Journal posted a commentary written by Arthur Laffer and Stephen Moore titled "A Win-Win Exit Strategy for Trump on Tariffs."

The proposal,

"Mr. Trump should give a globally televised address announcing to the world that the U.S. is ready to drop its tariffs and industry subsidies to zero tomorrow on any nation that does the same."

"This would be the ultimate reciprocal tariff policy."

On April 9, 2025, "Trump Authorizes 90-day pause on Reciprocal Tariffs."

In the Wall Street Journal, we read,

"Trump said his decision to pause the tariffs was based on the fact that more than 75 countries have engaged with representatives of his administration to open negotiations regarding trade, trade barriers, tariffs, currency manipulation, and other matters. He said that those companies also had not retaliated to earlier tariffs that Trump had threatened."

This decision, according to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, "wasn't driven by turmoil in the financial markets but was instead part of Trump's long-term strategy."

Going back to the Laffer/Moore piece in the Wall Street Journal, it seems as if there seems to be some kind of planning going on here.

Laffer has worked for Donald Trump.

Mr. Laffer's political life goes back to the early 1970s and started out as the Chief Economist at the Office of Management and Budget for Secretary George Shultz.

Mr. Laffer's life has been full of political involvement and lots of direct contact with presidents, cabinet secretaries, and other major players in the political process.

His advice is closely listened to.

In 2019, President Trump saw that Mr. Laffer received the Presidential Medal of Freedom for his work and advice.

My point here is that the timing of these two events may give us some hint into the "true" direction Trump is going on the issue of tariffs.

Laffer and Moore point out that Mr. Trump, in 2018, at a meeting of the Group of Seven Min Charlevoix, Quebec made this proposition to the "world's leaders":

"The U.S. would lower its tariffs to zero if their nations would do the same."

More explicitly,

"No tariffs, no barriers. That's the way it should be. And no subsidies. I even said, 'no tariffs'...."

The argument made by Laffer and Moore is that Mr. Trump has brought the world to its current position to

"pull off what could become the greatest Art of the Deal negotiation in world history."

"It would restore a free, fair, and unfettered global trading system."

"Everyone, everywhere, would get richer."

"The American economy would be great again."

"And, Donald Trump would win the 2025 Nobel Peace Prize."

Wow!!!

Mr. Laffer has remained close to Mr. Trump.

He has written two books specifically relating to Mr. Trump's economic successes and one book on the tax history of the United States. They are:

1. "Trumponomics: Inside the America First Plan to Revive Our Economy" with Stephen Moore;

2. "Taxes Have Consequences: A Tax History of the United States," with Brian Domitrovic and Jean Sinquefield;

3. "The Trump Economic Miracle," with Stephen Moore.

The first book was published in 2018.

The second book was published in 2022 and contains a foreword by Donald Trump.

The third book was published in 2024 and contains the argument that Donald Trump's pro-growth policies fueled unprecedented growth and prosperity."

It would seem as if Mr. Laffer would know Mr. Trump's mind about as well as anybody. Laffer has advised Mr. Trump over many years...while Mr. Trump was a candidate for president...while Mr. Trump was president...and while Mr. Trump built up his run for a second term as president. And now, while Mr. Trump is serving his second term as president.

It appears to me that the opinion piece written by Mr. Laffer and Mr. Moore, which appeared in the April 8, 2025, edition of the Wall Street Journal, certainly seems connected in one way or another to the announcement on April 9, 2025, by Mr. Trump.

If this is true, then certainly one can make the argument that Mr. Trump knew...and planned...his next step...the pause...and that there is more to come as Mr. Trump attempts to "pull off what could become the greatest Art of the Deal negotiation in world history...."

data.title: Osisko Development: Permits In Place, Construction Begins, And CEO Seeks Financing
data.description:
A “Hold” Rating for Osisko Development Corp.

This analysis confirms the “Hold” recommendation for the NYSE-listed shares of Osisko Development Corp. (ODV), a Montreal, Canada-based gold exploration and development company with the goal of one day developing a gold mine from its flagship Cariboo Gold Project in British Columbia, Canada.

Since the Previous Rating of “Hold”

Since our last article on June 26, 2024, NYSE-listed shares of Osisko Development Corp. – referred to simply as “Osisko” or “ODV” – have seen a negative change of -22.11% compared to -0.07% change for the S&P 500 Index.

This underperformance does not reflect the progress the company has made with its flagship gold mining project in British Columbia. Following the receipt of the approval under the BC Mines Act on November 20, 2024, the company can now proceed with the construction and operation of the Cariboo underground gold project. Meanwhile, other prospectors are also showing interest in the same mining area, which also makes us optimistic about the Osisko project.

Capital must be raised, and this will be done through borrowing as existing cash is insufficient. We believe financing costs will be a key factor in changing sentiment toward these shares, but they could remain bearish until the Federal Reserve returns to a rate-cut policy.

Given this pressure on Osisko's market value, we had recommended that investors wait but not sell, as the outlook for the Cariboo Gold project has actually improved, and only increase their positions after a significant decline in the share price, i.e., a "dip."

Two price dips occurred on November 15, 2024, and February 27, 2025, as a positive correlation with the gold price pushed ODV shares into a strongly bearish mood. The first dip was triggered by the “biggest weekly decline in months.”, as the Federal Reserve took its time deciding on future interest rate cuts amid strong US economic growth, Seeking Alpha reported. The second dip was triggered by “the deepest pullback since mid-December.”, “as investors price in inflation risks caused by potential new tariffs”, Seeking Alpha wrote.

We believe that the investments would have performed significantly better if they had benefited from these price dips.

Cariboo Gold District Attracts Capital and the Interest of Other Gold Prospectors

The Cariboo Gold Project covers approximately 192,000 hectares and comprises 415 mineral titles in British Columbia, Canada, 63 km east of Quesnel, British Columbia, Canada.

Osisko Development Corp.'s Cariboo Gold Project borders six adjacent Green River Gold Corp. (OTCPK:CCRRF) placer claims totaling 255.94 hectares, which abut the Swift River, a river with “a significant history of placer mining production dating back to the Cariboo Gold Rush in the 1870's.”, Green River says, where it decided to resume operations in June 2024.

Green River will leverage its commercial viability to raise funds for the development of the Quesnel Nickel Project, located in the same Cariboo mineral district. Green River will also utilize debt financing, and with respect to this source of capital, the company will raise CA$900,000 (approximately US$635,000 at the time of this article) through the issuance of five-year convertible debentures, as announced in early September 2024. This debt carries a costly interest rate of 11% per year. Of course, this is still a modest amount of capital compared to an industry like gold mining, which typically requires much higher investments, but such efforts nevertheless raise a strong suspicion: operators in this region, including Osisko, may have got gold deposits that, if converted into productive mines, could be very profitable. Since the goal was to blow up the stages, even at the cost of an annual interest rate significantly above the market level, the benchmark for which could be the five-year U.S. Treasury yield of 4.03% on Thursday, April 10, there should be little doubt that the rise in the gold price was a decisive factor in Green River's financing decision. However, without wishing to diminish the emphasis of the ongoing upward trend in the price of gold, it is also true that the decision to take on this burdensome debt was made long before the yellow metal broke the $3,000 per ounce mark in mid-March 2025.

Green River's decision to seek debt financing is likely to be welcomed, particularly by those who believe that the Cariboo Gold District has excellent prospects of becoming a thriving gold-producing region that generates robust cash. There are two reasons for believing this, which we have identified as follows:

FIRST, the region is very rich in gold and other metals, and operators like Green River, apparently, can hardly wait to implement their growth plans.

Cariboo's Mineral Resources and Exploration Potential and the QR Mine Deposit Area

These aspects highlighted in the “ FIRST” point above bode well for:

The reliability of Osisko's mineral reserves and resources in the Cariboo (see screenshot below) and Quesnel River Mine Project (or "QR Mine Project").

Cariboo's exploration potential is open in all directions, especially at depth, and according to Osisko, surpasses “Selected Canadian Operating Underground Mines” such as Alamos Gold Inc.'s (AGI) Young Davidson, Agnico Eagle Mines Limited's (AEM) Goldex, and LaRonde Zone 5 (see screenshot below).

The QR Mine deposit area— “located approximately 58 kilometers ("km") South-East from the city of Quesnel, accessible year-round, and approximately 111 km from the Cariboo Gold Project.”, according to Osisko — is a gold skarn deposit. Scientific literature suggests that the high-grade gold skarn deposits (5-15 g/t Au) may harbor undiscovered precious metal potential, are not widely distributed, and are primarily used for the extraction of the yellow metal, while other metals are present in concentrations unfavorable for extraction. In our view, since there are no economic incentives to mine metals other than gold, processing focuses on extracting a specific metal from the mineralized material, and the scope for exploration and exploitation will be less intrusive. These targeted production activities can result in reduced environmental impacts, increase the chances of obtaining all mining permits, and are likely to be among the most cost-effective assets.

QR Mine Prospect appears to be one of these high-grade, profitable, cost-effective gold skarn deposits. This is based on the results of the initial drilling campaign in 2024, designed to validate data from previous exploration activities (from the 1970s to 2000s). QR Mine Prospect-High Grade gold skarn deposits association is also based on the operating results of the then operator, Kinross Gold Corporation (KGC), which mined 1.06 million tonnes of gold at 4.1 g/t Au between 1994 and 1998 and produced a total of 118,004 ounces of gold over the mine life.

SECOND, the mineral formations in which these deposits occur at Cariboo Gold and in the surrounding area are such that their extraction typically requires significantly less effort than currently by gold producers worldwide.

Future Production and Estimated Costs Compared to the Actual Costs Of the Global Industry

Green River is targeting solid cash flow from its placer mining assets near the Cariboo Gold Project. As Perry Little, President and CEO of Green River, stated on June 24 of last year, as reported by Yahoo Finance:

“ "With gold prices at current levels, we see the possibility of creating significant cash flow from our placer mining assets. […] so that any money we raise in the capital markets goes directly to […] Our primary focus […] the Quesnel Nickel Project […] In the meantime, any mining activity that produces cash flow for the Company benefits all our shareholders." ”

Regarding Osisko's cash flow prospects, investors can rely on the continued strong upward momentum in the gold price as well as an All-In-Sustaining Cost estimate that makes the Cariboo Gold Project a very cost-effective gold mine compared to global operators.

Osisko plans to produce 164,000 ounces of gold over a twelve-year operating life at the Cariboo Gold Project. The project consists of five main zones (Shaft Zone, Valley Zone, Cow Zone, Mosquito Zone, and Lowhee Zone, as well as the QR Mill, an existing facility that is under care and maintenance). AISC is US$968 per ounce, decreasing to US$886 per ounce starting in year four (the start of the second production phase). Osisko's Cariboo Gold Project will be a highly cost-effective gold production compared to the overall gold production industry, which is approaching US$1,500 per ounce (see below chart).

Low AISC And Bullish Gold Price Boost Key Economics of Cariboo

The price of gold is expected to contribute significantly to the profitability of the Cariboo Gold Project, as the price per ounce has confirmed its territory among historical highs to date due to strong demand for safe haven assets amid geopolitical tensions, including the trade war.

Following a 19.03% year-to-date and 31.64% year-on-year increase, the Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) is at US$ 3,123.68/oz at the time of this writing. UBS and Macquarie have raised their price forecasts to US$3,200/ounce and US$3,500/ounce, respectively. Last month, Goldman Sachs and Bank of America also raised their gold price forecasts to US$ 3,300/oz (from US$ 3,100/oz) by 2025 and US$ 3,500/oz within the next two years, respectively.

This gold price trend, coupled with the forecasts of analysts at the US bank giants, entitles us to the most valuable section of the economic estimates in the 2023 profitability study. These suggest that, assuming an average gold price of US$2,000 per ounce—which is itself a conservative assumption compared to the current gold price and projected performance—Cariboo has a net present value (“NPV”) of CA$821 million (approximately US$579.1 million at the time of writing), an NPV of CA$6.00 per share (approximately US$4.24 per share), and an Internal Rate of Return (“IRR”) of 31.4%. ODV's current share price is $1.48 per share, representing a market capitalization of $202.81 million as of this writing. The internal rate of return (IRR) for other gold development projects, according to our previous analysis, is between 15 and 35 percent. The upside potential for the ODV stock price is enormous, and the markets cannot stand idly by for long.

Consistent with our previous analysis, we believe that the resumption of central bank rate cuts, which market participants first got a taste of in late 2024, will allow for a much-needed gradual easing of the credit crunch forced to combat runaway inflation, and in our view this will be the signal that many are waiting for before plunging back into the ODV market.

As developments from prospectors in and around the Cariboo Gold Project area continue to bolster ODV's growth and low AISC/oz prospects for Osisko's future gold production, and as the gold price keeps on rising, all of these things happening as we approach the return of interest rate cuts, ODV shares are increasingly loaded with momentum ready to unleash a recovery in the share price. Driven by fears of a tariff war-triggered recession in the US and the associated consequences for the US stock market, Fed interest rate traders now expect up to five interest rate cuts of 25 basis points each by the end of the year and a probability of over 50% that the Fed will make its first interest rate cut at its next meeting on June 18, 2025.

The Cariboo Gold project continues to gain momentum, as the company, strengthened by the BC Mines Act permits received on November 20, 2024, can now begin construction and operation of the Cariboo underground gold asset. To this end, Osisko Works is conducting an optimized feasibility study, which is expected to be completed in the second quarter of 2025.

All these positive developments, along with the exciting presence of Green River Gold Corp. nearby, are prompting Osisko to further boost its financial position through debt financing. The financial position consisted of CA$106.7 million in cash and cash equivalents and a low debt of $36.0 million (US$25.0 million) as of the end of Q4 2024. Mining.com reported in late 2024 that Osisko CEO Sean Roosen was seeking a $750 million financing package to accelerate the project.

Osisko Development Corp. also owns the Tintic Project – Utah, USA (100% owned) and the San Antonio Gold Project – Sonora State, Mexico (100% owned), but there has been no significant news since our last article.

The Stock Price: Stick to Hold Rating and Buy If RSI Signals Oversold

At the time of writing, ODV shares were trading on the New York Stock Exchange at $1.48 per share, representing a market capitalization of $202.81 million.

The shares are trading below the midpoint of $1.955 per share of the 52-week range of $1.16 to $2.75 per share but are not completely below the MA Ribbon.

The 14-day Relative Strength Indicator of 51.88 is also well above the oversold level, which we recommend in this analysis based on past trends as an indicator of a “True Dip” that investors could use to occasionally switch from "Hold" to "Buy."

Inflation concerns due to the tariff war could trigger the next dip.

The Fed is sticking to its plan to make only two 25 basis point interest rate cuts in the second half of 2025. Instead, the market is optimistic, predicting five 25 basis point interest rate cuts starting in June.

If inflation rises in the short term as panicked consumers drive up prices for fear of shortages on their shelves, refrigerators, and pantries, the Fed could reaffirm its cautious stance. The market would be disappointed, and since the zero-yielding gold price will be exposed to a negative impact, ODV shares could collapse.

Conclusion

Osisko Development Corp. is advancing its flagship gold project in British Columbia – the Cariboo Gold Project. A feasibility study indicates a 12-year production target of 1.87 million ounces of gold and very low AISC per ounce.

Until central banks lower borrowing costs, the lack of financing will weigh on sentiment unless Osisko, like other gold prospectors in the region, is willing to borrow at expensive rates. Market participants know the company needs capital to build the mine. Osisko has received all the necessary permits to build the gold mine, which has helped maintain sentiment afloat and prevent excessive deterioration.

The company is seeking financing, but the central bank's shift from a “higher-for-longer” to a dovish monetary policy could significantly boost the project and, with it, the share price.

The Cariboo Gold Mine will produce at a low cost and, with such an astonishing rally in the gold price, could become one of the most profitable in the world.

Despite good progress, including the approval of the permits, the situation remains uncertain. Investors should, therefore, maintain the “Hold” rating, and only price dips could be seen as opportunities to increase their holdings.

data.title: Global Trade And Tariffs 2.0: Tightly Woven Supply Chain Tapestry
data.description:
In sorting through recent tariff pronouncements, while fluid, some lessons from the 2018-2019 U.S.-China tariffs of the first Trump administration are worth considering. On April 10, the Dallas Federal Reserve Bank held a conference about North American trade and the outlook, in conjunction with the think tank Peterson Institute for International Economics. In the 11th "Fed" district, the region's GDP is largely from Texas. We are smack in the middle of the trade and tariff impacts. Thus, some findings are instructive. The lessons from the tariffs of the above-mentioned period are notable textbook economics, but the world economy and security emphasis has changed too since the end of the pandemic.

One of the more glaring takeaways is that North America is a highly integrated market owing to trade, with unquestionable competitive advantages that are costly to upend and bend.

Some further general North America trade color:

• Canada and Mexico together account for over a third of total U.S. agricultural exports and over 40% of total U.S. agricultural imports.

• That’s total back and forth North American trade of almost $140 billion, with a U.S. trade deficit of about $32 billion.

As below, Canada and Mexico together account for around $66.7 billion of U.S. energy exports and $141 billion of total U.S. energy imports. The top line is crude petroleum and petroleum products traded with Canada, which supplies the U.S., with a deficit of about $91 billion last year. Note: we import their heavy crude and refine it, owing to the composition of U.S. oil grades/types and Midwest-located refining capacity. Canada is roughly 60% of U.S. oil imports, an important energy security trading relationship.

Small numbers can be important, however, one economist pointed out. The U.S. imports $2.3 billion of electricity from Canada, hence the retaliatory threat months ago, with negative effects on a few U.S. states.

U.S.-China tariff war

The following highlights the impacts of the last "trade war." I will highlight key points and their meaning:

The U.S. tariffs on China of 2018-19 are revealed through Mexico’s exports and the nearshoring story. The reshuffling of supply chains has been happening through alternative models, those that are more asset light. One is the shelter model, which is sort of like renting a factory, and another one is contracting out. These trends “show up” through the exports of Mexico, not through foreign direct investment. Tariffs were integrated into supply chains, and they were largely focused on intermediate and capital goods.

That means firms that increased trade after the ’18-19 tariffs, aimed at China, tended to be weaved into the supply chain tapestry. Further, Mexico has shown to have competitive advantages in chemicals, machinery and some agriculture, further strengthened by the shifting of activity.

Most of Mexico's export gains tended to be associated with foreign-owned maquilas.

Bottom line: Some of the activity in Mexico, post-U.S. tariffs targeting China, is picked up in the maquilas activity. The research shows that Mexico is importing more from Southeast Asian countries and China as a redress to tariffs though the channel of foreign-owned maquilas.

Basically, the nearshoring story with Mexico is a proxy for and substitution of China's exports. The trade war between the US and China led to an increase in export activity for Mexico. The trade war was also concentrated in sectors where competitive advantage was already occurring, and thus exploited more.

Other trade notes

Generally, China is also increasing its trade more toward emerging economies. That trend was happening before the tariff war. Additionally, a China-plus-1 strategy is occurring: If firms have production in China, the hedge is to add production elsewhere as a way to diversify your supply chain. Publicly traded announcements from Apple (AAPL) and many others bear this out, too.

Since China's entry into the World Trade Organization, trade imbalances between the U.S. and China have been like a mirror image to each other.

Interestingly, U.S. imports have now tilted more toward advanced economies (ex. Canada), Southeast Asia (ex. China), and Mexico. Again, we are importing more from China via Mexico from the production shifts, owing to tariffs.

Texas is highly impacted by trade and tariffs, globally, and with Mexico, especially with the maquilas factories, defined as those with freer trade or duty free. Note below that Texas is globally competitive in mining, which is oil and gas, computers and electronics, services and furniture.

Another key takeaway is that trade between the U.S. and Mexico is a round-trip prospect. Thus, tariffs on Mexico or Canada include U.S. content.

U.S. integration of manufacturing with Canada and Mexico is unique relative to other trading partners, notes the economist Flaaen at the Fed. Further:

“We find that the overall impact of these tariffs was actually negative on US manufacturing, specifically through rising input cost channel, making inputs more expensive, hurting U.S. manufacturing competitiveness. This outweighed the positive impacts that you might traditionally think would be true of the impact of the US manufacturing sector.

Here again, the tariffs predominantly affected intermediate inputs, as noted:

“So those intermediate inputs were directly leading to higher costs for manufacturing, that at least in the short run, served to weaken the sector rather than strengthen it.”

Also, generally about how the tariffs filtered through:

…Producer prices begin turning up pretty much right away when the tariffs were implemented. But one critical feature is that it wasn't a one-off jump in prices and then things go back down. Things gradually move up for a period of about nine to 12 months as tariffs were implemented.”

More color on Mexico and Canada trade:

“Motor vehicles plays a pretty strong role—what U.S manufacturing relies on coming from Mexico. Other areas like machinery and electrical equipment are also important in how U.S manufacturing relates to Mexico. With Canada, U.S. industries are reliant on high-energy content goods, like aluminum but also pulp paper, wood products and fertilizer.

For every dollar imported from Mexico and Canada, about 20 cents is actually U.S. content, notes Flaaen. He sums it up: "Tariffs on Canada and Mexico have unique repercussions for the U.S. manufacturing sector. What I like to say is that retaliation is actually built-in when we put tariffs on Canadian and Mexican imports because it's already (going) to affect U.S. content."

In summary

The complexity of the North America trade story, while unique, has lessons for other inherently bound trading blocs, like the European Union and Southeast Asia. While bilateral trade deals have meaning, interwoven supply chains may mean more in the long run, depending on the value and strategic importance of the industry. Also imperative: what's a valuable industry worth protecting based on national security grounds, and where does it make sense to work with allies, a far less punitive prospect? Nevertheless, while markets and competitive advantages matter, firms' choices and workarounds happen, either adding cost or refashioning market share in possibly distorting or advantageous ways. It's complicated, but partners and the nature of partnerships matter.

(Note: Pardon the expediency of information here vs. prose.)

data.title: AptarGroup: Strong Pharma And Closure Growth To Offset Beauty Headwinds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

AptarGroup, Inc. (NYSE:ATR) is poised to continue benefiting from growth in its Pharma and Closure segments, while the Beauty segment should present some challenges in the upcoming quarters. On the margin side, the company should benefit from cost-saving initiatives and volume leverage in FY2025. Given the anticipated revenue growth, margin expansion, and the stock’s discount relative to its 5-year P/E ratio, I recommend a buy rating.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

In FY2024, ATR reported $3.58 billion in revenue, which was up 3% year-over-year, driven by favorable product mix and volume growth. In the fourth quarter of FY24, the Pharma segment’s core sales increased by 4%, driven by healthy demand in the proprietary drug systems business (up 15% Y/Y). However, the growth was partially offset by injectable and consumer healthcare business. Injectable business was down 8% year-over-year due to lower service and tooling revenue. Moreover, the consumer healthcare business’s core sales decreased 17% year-over-year due to weaker 2023-24 cold flu season and inventory management at the customer level. In the Q4 24, the beauty segment’s core sales decreased 3% year-over-year in the quarter, largely due to lower tooling sales as well as lower sales of higher value prestige products. The core sales of the closure segment in Q4 were increased by 7% year-over-year, driven by increased demand across a number of end markets, including food and beverage.

Looking ahead, I believe the company should continue to benefit from its pharma and closure segment. However, these positive impacts on the top-line should be partially offset by the weakness in the beauty segment of the company.

The prescription drug business, which accounts for 49% of the pharma segment, is poised for year-over-year revenue growth, driven by its proprietary drug delivery devices. ATR designs and manufactures the unidose nasal system for Neffy and the BioDose nasal delivery system for Spravato. Recently, the FDA approved Spravato as a monotherapy treatment, allowing it to be used independently without the need for additional oral solid dose drugs to treat depression. Additionally, both the FDA and the European Medicines Agency have approved Neffy and EURneffy, which deliver epinephrine via nasal spray. With the introduction of new drugs to the market, these regulatory approvals are expected to serve as a growth catalyst for ATR in the upcoming quarters. Moreover, in the pharma segment, the injectable business manufactures elastomeric primary packaging components that facilitate the administration of injected medicines. The management expects service and tooling revenue from this business to remain uncertain in the coming quarters. Nevertheless, the component side of the injectable business is well positioned to serve the growing GLP-1 demand in the market. GLP-1 is used for weight loss and to treat diabetes, which is administrated through subcutaneous injection. Over the years, there have been significant changes in the overweight and obesity category of the population of the USA. Almost 64% of the population is considered to be either overweight or obese in 2025, compared to 60% in 2015. Moreover, there have been significant changes in the class 3 obesity, from 4.9% in 2015 to 6.1% in 2025. Such demography of the population well bodes for the injectable business of ATR. Although the component sales are expected to grow, I believe the lower service & tooling revenue should more than offset these benefits on the top-line Y/Y growth in the coming quarters.

ATR’s beauty segment (represents 34% of its net sales) provides a broad variety of pumps, airless systems and values to fragrance, color cosmetics, facial skincare, and personal care. The beauty segment is expected to report a revenue decline year over year in the coming quarters. This decrease is primarily attributed to lower sales of high-value prestige products. In the current economic climate, characterized by worsening macroeconomic conditions and the looming recession, consumers are increasingly shifting their preferences towards masstige products. Masstige products come with an appeal of luxury with more accessible price points, which aligns better with the spending habits of consumers who are prioritizing value for money amidst financial uncertainty.

ATR’s closures segment, which accounts for approximately 17% of its net sales, focuses on dispensing closures for food, beverage, and personal care products. Over the past year, global consumers have increasingly favored packaging that prioritizes convenience, driving demand for features like flow control and no-drip dispensing. ATR is also evolving its product offerings towards market-fit innovations such as lighter-weight custom closures and the SimpliSqueeze Value line, designed for easy, single-handed use without leakage. Recent developments include the custom Flip-Top closures featured in McCormick Grill Mates spices and Holiday Sugars in the U.S. and the launch of adult-powered milk products with lighter-weight custom closures by Nestlé in Asia. With these new product offerings and evolving market trends, the segment is expected to achieve year-over-year revenue growth in the coming quarters.

Margin analysis and outlook

ATR’s adjusted EBITDA margin reached 21.6% in FY2024, reflecting a 130 basis point increase from 20.3% in FY2023. This improvement was driven by operational leverage, a favorable mix of higher-value products, and effective cost efficiency measures.

For the Pharma segment, the adjusted EBITDA margin for the quarter was 35.7%, marking a 160 basis point gain compared to the prior year. This growth was supported by higher sales of premium products and services, including royalties, alongside cost efficiency initiatives. In contrast, the Beauty segment experienced a decline in its adjusted EBITDA margin to 12.4%, down 230 basis points year-over-year. This drop was primarily due to volume deleverage, particularly in higher-priced prestige products. The Closure segment saw an adjusted EBITDA margin of 16.1%, reflecting a 260 basis point improvement over the same period last year. This growth was largely driven by volume expansion and effective cost and productivity management."

On the margin front, I Believe the operational leverage, cost management initiatives and plant utilization should contribute to improved adjusted EBITDA margin in the coming quarters. In the Beauty segment of the company, the manufacturing plants have been reduced by 10 in the last two years and workforce by 11%. The company is restructuring to optimize its economies of scale, which should continue to positively impact the adjusted EBITDA margin.

In addition to cost reduction benefits, the Pharma segment is expected to contribute to ATR’s margin expansion. Within this segment, the injectable business relies on stringent quality standards to consistently meet demanding regulatory requirements. Products with superior quality leads to premium pricing, and this favorable mix of higher-value products should enhance the company’s overall margin performance.

Valuation & Conclusion

ATR is currently trading at 25.88x forward P/E, with a consensus EPS estimate of $5.55 for FY25. During the recent earnings call, the company provided an EPS outlook for Q1 FY2025 in the range of $1.11 to $1.19, which includes a 7-cent impact from currency translation and an 8-cent tax headwind. My EPS estimate aligns with the consensus, reflecting low-single-digit revenue growth compared to the previous year.

The stock is trading at approximately a 15% discount to its 5-year average forward P/E ratio of 30.23x, though it remains at a premium compared to the sector median P/E ratio of 12.81x. Considering the company's revenue growth, margin expansion, and the discount to its historical P/E, I believe ATR presents an attractive investment opportunity. Therefore, I recommend a buy rating on the stock. Based on the 5-year forward P/E ratio of 30.23x and the consensus EPS estimate, I estimate a target price of $168 for investors.

Risk

ATR's operations may be adversely affected by geopolitical tensions, trade disputes, international sanctions or boycotts, political and social unrest, acts of war, terrorism, or other similar disruptive events. These circumstances could hinder its ability to manufacture or deliver products to customers, source materials from suppliers, or carry out essential business functions. Such disruptions may impact its performance globally or in specific regions.

data.title: Athabasca Oil Corporation: Strong Cash Flow, Low Valuation, High Upside
data.description:
Despite the fact that the oil price has fallen to about $60 per barrel, Athabasca Oil Corporation (OTCPK:ATHOF)(TSX:ATH:CA) remains one of the most attractive value investments in the Canadian energy sector. With an impeccable balance sheet, low capital needs, 90+ years of reserves, and an aggressive capital repayment program, the company offers an asymmetrical risk and return profile.

Operations

Athabasca produces about 37,000 BOE/D, 95% of which consists of high marginal bitumen (heavy oil) from its long-term oil sand deposits. With main projects like Leismer and Hangingstone, the company can maintain high activity margins even at $60 per barrel.

The management stated that the company's break-even is about $40 WTI. At $60, the company still generates free cash flow, fully funding investment and the repurchase of shares. In contrast to shale companies, oil sand deposits have a very slow decline in extraction, so little investment is required to maintain production levels.

In 2024, capital spending was around CAD268 million, mainly dedicated to the development of Leismer and drilling in the Duvernay deposit. Even when investing in growth, the company generated CAD322 million free cash flow, which means more than 12% FCF yield.

Shareholder Value

From 2021, Athabasca returned more than $400 million of debt and now has a cash excess of about $145 million. Interest costs are low, and the remaining USD157 million debt can be fully covered without refinancing (the debt must be paid by 2026).

The company does not consider this money to be part of the reserve. In 2024 alone, it repurchased about $317 million in shares - more than 10% of their shares in circulation. The management promised to return 100% of the free cash flow to the shareholders in 2025 as well. The number of shares decreased from ~584 million in 2023 to ~518 million in 2024.

Valuation: Cheap

  • EV/EBITDA (2024): ~4.5x

  • P/E (2024): ~5.9x

  • FCF yield (2024): ~12%

  • P/B: ~1.3x, with 25%+ ROE

Most importantly, the net present value (NPV10) in the 2P reserves report is CAD6.4 billion - This is CAD12.44 per share when the share price is only around CAD 5. Even with risk and taxes, the market values ​​these shares at a huge discount.

The value of developed reserves (PDP) alone exceeds the entire company's enterprise value. This is the margin of safety that most E&P companies do not have.

Macroeconomics And Athabasca

At first glance, $60 per barrel seems bad. However, heavy oil producers in 2024 and 2025 had quite good news.

  • The differences between WTI-WCS narrowed to historic lows, sometimes reaching <10 USD
  • OPEC+ restrictions and sanctions for Russia/Venezuela restricted heavy oil supply.
  • Launching TMX Pipeline in 2025 added 590 thousand BBL/D export power, which is a structural improvement in prices.

Athabasca has resulted in the improvement of bituminous oil realization, even at lower WTI prices. In Q4 2024, its netback was CA 46 per barrel compared to just ~CAD30 a year earlier.

This is an invisible factor for gaining profit, which is often forgotten by investors. Even at $60, Athabasca generates strong profits.

Athabasca has 90+ years of 2P reserves, including big potential. Leismer development to reach up to 40,000 BOE/D is already being implemented.

The company also owns the Corner Field (capacity of 40,000 BBL/D) that could be developed or sold. Its Duvernay joint venture with Cenovus grows towards 6,000 BOE/D in 2025. These long-term opportunities are not yet priced in.

Tariffs

US rates: a short-term problem, perhaps even a better thing in the long term. In April 2025, the US announced a 10% tariff on certain Canadian energy products, including bitumen oil, the primary product of Athabasca Oil Corporation. It was part of a broader trade conflict over electric car subsidies. Some investors responded to this panic, and let's be honest, we had a selloff. Keep in mind that even if they are lifted, U.S. trade policy has proven volatile, so stocks like this may remain sensitive to headlines.

These tariffs can reduce the price by approximately $2-3 per barrel. Because Athabasca sells about 37 thousand daily, it could theoretically "cut" about 5-7% of profit - not nice, but far from a disaster. Especially knowing that their costs of doing business are very low and that free cash flow is still strong even at $60 per barrel.

Another thing is that Athabasca is not stuck in the US market. From 2024, the Trans Mountain Expansion (TMX) pipeline began to export heavy oil to Asia - China, Japan, and South Korea. Not only does it reduce the dependence on the US, but it also opens the door for higher prices where there are simply no tariffs.

Thus, from the investor's position, tariffs are more a temporary inconvenience than a real threat. And if tariffs stay, Athabasca has alternatives. If they disappear, we will have very clear catalysts for share price growth, and given this industry lobbying, it is very likely. Some investors see the risk here, but others see a chance. Emotions often create "sales" on the market - which is just a moment to enter.

Conclusion

The market often responds too much to short-term noises. Oil falls to $60, tariffs are announced, and investors are panicking. But those who look deeper see something else. Athabasca Oil Corporation is a classic case where the market overestimates risks and underestimates value.

This is not a speculative company that burns money and waits for the oil price to bounce. This is a company that is already profitable. With ~ $40 at a break-even point, more than 12% of free cash flow and 90 years reserves are a strong, efficient machine that generates money even under current conditions. It has small debt and excess cash of over 140 million, and in 2024 alone, it bought shares for more than $300 million.

Tariffs? Yes, they are unpleasant, but they do not change the essence of the business model. In addition, the TMX pipeline has already opened the door to Asian markets - where there are no rates and prices are often better. Athabasca has strategic freedom that many competitors would be jealous.

In the long run, here is what the value investors call "free options" - you can't pay for growth, but you still get it. Even if the oil remains at $60, Athabasca still works. If the price rises to $75, the shares can jump to a double-digit percentage.

My rating for it currently stands at BUY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tariffs Take Aim At Amazon: Navigating 'Liberation Day' Shock
data.description:
The Trump administration’s April 2025 “Liberation Day” tariffs represent a seismic shift in U.S. trade policy, imposing sweeping duties on imports from virtually all major trade partners. The plan introduced a baseline 10% tariff on all imports to the U.S. (effective April 5), with even higher rates on 57 targeted countries – including 20% on the EU, 26% on India, 24% on Japan, and a combined 125% on China when layered on prior China-specific tariffs.

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), as a global e-commerce titan and a major cloud provider, faces a uniquely multifaceted exposure to this tariff storm. On the retail side, Amazon’s marketplace is deeply entwined with international supply chains: over 60% of its retail sales come from third-party sellers, many of whom source products from Asia and around the world. Amazon’s own first-party retail operations also import vast volumes of consumer goods into the U.S., from electronics to apparel, meaning the new import taxes could directly inflate its cost of goods sold.

Simultaneously, Amazon Web Services (AWS) – which leads the $200+ billion cloud industry – could become a collateral victim if trade partners respond with digital trade barriers or if economic uncertainty dampens corporate tech spending. Amazon’s extensive logistics and fulfillment infrastructure, spanning ports, warehouses, and delivery networks across continents, further illustrates its reliance on fluid global trade. The company operates over 1 billion square feet of fulfillment space worldwide (versus about 176 million for Walmart’s (WMT) e-commerce operations), a network designed to source and ship products efficiently across borders.

Tariff Mechanics & Amazon’s Operations

For Amazon’s 1P (first-party) retail business, this means many products it purchases from overseas manufacturers will suddenly cost more. In practice, Amazon faces a tough choice: absorb these added costs (hurting its profit margins), pass them onto consumers as higher prices (risking lower sales), or negotiate sharing the pain with suppliers. Amazon is already reportedly pressing some vendors to reduce their wholesale prices to offset tariffs. Its immense scale and buying power – it can leverage being many suppliers’ largest customer – may allow Amazon to claw back some concessions. However, vendors too are squeezed by the tariffs, and not all cost increases can be bargained away.

Shipping and fulfillment costs may also rise under tariff pressures. If certain foreign sourcing becomes uneconomical, Amazon might need to shift orders to alternative countries or domestic suppliers, potentially at higher base prices. Such reconfiguration isn’t instantaneous; as supply chain expert Marc Wulfraat notes, Amazon cannot easily “re-configure its supply chain meaningfully in short order” since it does not control many of its international suppliers (especially for third-party sellers). In the near term, Amazon could try to expedite imports before tariffs take full effect or draw down existing inventory stocked in the U.S. (indeed, Amazon reportedly had about 4 weeks of inventory on hand for many goods, which might delay the immediate impact). But once pre-tariff stockpiles run down by mid-quarter, Amazon’s operating costs on newly imported goods will reflect the tariff-driven inflation.

E-Commerce & Logistics Exposure

Amazon’s core e-commerce business – both the direct sales (1P) and the third-party marketplace (3P) – is highly exposed to tariff impacts, especially in discretionary product categories. We estimate a whopping ~87% of Amazon’s U.S. gross merchandise volume (GMV) is linked to products that could be hit by tariffs. This includes most non-essential items: electronics, apparel, toys, home goods, and more, which Amazon sources globally or hosts on its platform via foreign sellers. In contrast, essentials like groceries (a category where Amazon’s presence is smaller relative to total sales) have more domestic sourcing and thus face less direct tariff pressure.

The 3P marketplace dynamics add another layer. Third-party sellers (who account for ~60% of units sold on Amazon) are themselves importers or resellers of imported goods in many cases. These entrepreneurs and businesses will face the new tariffs when bringing inventory into the U.S., and their ability to manage these costs varies. Many 3P sellers from China, for instance, operate on thin margins and a 125% tariff would be devastating. For Amazon, one immediate concern is that higher costs will curtail third-party sellers’ activity, leading to lower unit volumes and possibly a reduced assortment on the platform.

Sellers may also cut their advertising spending on Amazon. A significant portion of Amazon’s ad revenue (from Sponsored Products, Sponsored Brands, etc.) comes from 3P sellers promoting their listings. If those sellers see sales slowing or margins shrinking, they are likely to pull back on ad bids to conserve budget. Analysts are indeed baking in lower advertising revenue growth for Amazon’s 3P segment due to tariffs. This is noteworthy because advertising is a high-margin business for Amazon; any softness there directly pressures operating profit.

Moreover, if prices across Amazon’s site inflate due to tariffs, demand elasticity could kick in – especially among price-sensitive consumers. Households with tighter budgets might reduce discretionary purchases on Amazon, or even cancel Prime memberships, if they feel they’re not getting the same value. Fewer Prime members would be an alarming trend for Amazon, as Prime not only drives frequent purchasing but also underpins services like Prime Video and Whole Foods integration. While a mass exodus is unlikely, even a slight uptick in churn or downtick in new Prime sign-ups could hurt Amazon’s long-term customer lifetime value.

Amazon’s vast fulfillment and logistics network also faces logistical and cost challenges. The company relies on a finely tuned flow of inventory from manufacturers worldwide into its fulfillment centers. With the tariff regime, Amazon may need to adjust its inventory strategies. We might see Amazon and its sellers front-loading inventory before higher tariffs hit (as happened in late 2018 ahead of scheduled tariff hikes, when importers rushed goods to U.S. ports).

AWS (Cloud Services) Vulnerabilities

Beyond general economic trends, retaliatory actions by foreign governments could directly target Amazon’s cloud business. Europe in particular has signaled that U.S. digital services are a point of leverage. The EU runs a large deficit in digital services trade with the U.S. (over €100 billion), and European officials have mused that “digital services could be Washington’s Achilles heel” in a trade confrontation. This suggests that European regulators might accelerate measures that had already been under discussion – for instance, digital services taxes (DSTs) or new rules under the EU’s Digital Markets Act – which could impose extra costs or constraints on AWS. A digital services tax, even a small percentage on revenues, would effectively act like a tariff on cloud services revenue in those countries.

Europe previously put DST plans on hold in favor of OECD negotiations, but the tariff clash could revive those efforts. Additionally, Europe or other regions could enforce stricter data localization mandates requiring that data of their citizens be stored and processed locally. AWS does build local data centers (it has AWS Europe regions in countries like Ireland, Germany, and France), but more onerous localization rules could force AWS to invest in even more infrastructure per country, raising costs. Countries could also push back on data transfers, citing the need to “shield” local data from an American company if trade relations sour – a non-tariff barrier that might complicate AWS’s unified cloud network.

Another vulnerability is hardware and infrastructure costs for AWS. While cloud services themselves aren’t imports, AWS’s data centers are built from vast amounts of servers, networking gear, and other hardware – much of which is manufactured in Asia. Tariffs on Chinese high-tech components or semiconductor products could raise AWS’s capital expenditures. If, say, certain server components now carry a 10–15% import tax, equipping new data centers or expanding existing ones becomes more expensive for Amazon. This could either compress AWS’s profit margins or prompt AWS to raise prices down the line (though cloud pricing is fiercely competitive, making price hikes difficult). There is also a risk of supply chain delays for data center equipment if trade tensions disrupt electronics supply lines. Any slowdown in AWS infrastructure rollout could hurt its ability to meet customer demand timely, though so far, Amazon has indicated it can manage capacity well.

Competition & Regulatory Pressure

In the U.S., Amazon’s main retail rival Walmart could prove relatively resilient during the tariff turbulence – and perhaps even capitalize on it. Walmart’s supply chain has a huge domestic component: it has thousands of stores that function as both sales points and distribution nodes, and over half of Walmart’s product volume (especially groceries and essentials) is sourced and sold locally via its stores. If consumers become more price-sensitive, Walmart’s “Everyday Low Price” promise and ability to offer in-store pickup for online orders could attract value-conscious shoppers. The net effect is that competition between Amazon and Walmart could intensify, with each leaning on their strengths (Amazon on selection and Prime convenience, Walmart on price and local presence) to retain customers amid cost pressures.

Looking globally, Amazon must watch local e-commerce upstarts in emerging markets, too. In India, for example, Amazon and Walmart-owned Flipkart dominate, but both rely on some imported goods (electronics, etc.). The Indian government, which has its own history of protectionist policies, might respond sympathetically to U.S. tariffs by promoting “Make in India” and favoring indigenous platforms or stricter e-commerce FDI rules that hamper Amazon. Similarly, in Latin America, MercadoLibre (MELI) could play up its regional roots to outcompete Amazon if U.S.-Latin trade friction increases.

Investor Implications

Tariffs have introduced fresh uncertainty into Amazon’s recovery trajectory and could translate into higher costs of goods by a few billion dollars. As a result, management may adopt a cautious tone in upcoming guidance for Q2 and full-year 2025, factoring in potential trade negotiation outcomes. This caution has already rattled investor sentiment, with the stock initially dipping amid concerns about slower top-line growth and tighter profit ranges.

Profitability remains the focal point. While Amazon’s margins have been improving since 2023, tariffs could compress retail margins even as AWS and advertising lift the overall mix. The scale of margin impact depends on whether Amazon absorbs most tariff costs or passes them along to customers. A decision to maintain low prices could dampen near-term profits but reinforce customer loyalty; conversely, broad price hikes might preserve margins while risking volume softness. Even so, existing warehouse automation, corporate headcount reductions, and other efficiencies offer a buffer, and I still project Amazon’s EBIT margin landing in the low-to-mid 11% range for 2025.

Conclusion

The April 2025 “Liberation Day” tariffs present a significant challenge for Amazon, impacting its supply chains, retail margins, and AWS growth, while potentially disrupting logistics efficiency and increasing consumer prices; however, Amazon’s vast scale, diversified operations, and adaptability position it uniquely to mitigate these pressures. Short-term tactical responses—including modest price increases, cost management, and supply chain optimization—will enable Amazon to maintain steady, albeit slower, growth and profitability amidst the trade tensions. Amazon’s handling of this situation will be closely observed as a gauge for broader e-commerce inflation and digital market friction, with expectations generally optimistic given its historical resilience. Investors may see temporary margin pressure and restrained international expansion, yet Amazon’s core franchises and robust financial strength ensure its long-term trajectory remains solid, allowing it to potentially emerge stronger when trade conditions eventually normalize.

data.title: Bellway: Buy For The Growth Story, Avoid For The Lack Of Passive Income
data.description:
Like many of its housebuilding peers, Bellway (BLWYY) (OTCPK:BLWYF) stock has seen its share price tumble since the Autumn Budget, and has found it difficult to recover since amidst the market's volatility surrounding President Trump's tariffs. With the stock now trading below its book value, Bellway may be worth taking a look at for its growth story, rather than its dividends.

Brick by Brick

Bellway reported a rather encouraging set of figures in its interim results. Completions continued their upward momentum (+11.9%) to 4,577, and was complemented by the uptick in its net private sales rate (0.51 vs 0.43), as well as the 0.4% increase in the Average Selling Price [ASP] to £310,581. As a consequence, revenue was up a healthy 12.3% to £1.43B.

This hardy increase flowed through to underlying gross profit, which rose 11.1% to £234MM, but was offset slightly by the 17bps decrease in underlying gross margin to c.16.4% due to residual cost inflation, longer construction timelines, and customer incentives. Nonetheless, the builder's underlying EBIT margin was steady at c.11.0% with underlying EBIT 11.9% higher at £157MM.

Underlying net finance expense was unchanged from the year before, at £18MM. Paired with a tax rate that was also steady, Bellway's underlying profit after tax was 11.7% better, at £107MM. As a result, underlying diluted EPS also jumped 11.9% to 89.6p. Alongside this, the board lifted its interim DPS by a whopping 31.3% to 21.0p, much to the music of shareholder's ears.

But most promisingly, management reiterated its guidance to build 8,500 homes in FY25. To sweeten the deal, it even set a target to build another c.9,200 homes by FY26, as the company lines up more capacity from its landbank. This should, therefore, lead to better returns for shareholders, as evidenced by the large leap in dividends recently.

To reflect this, Bellway's order book grew yet again, to a total of 5,582 homes － its highest since H1 '23. And if its guidance is anything to go by, this should continue to build over the coming years, with the scope of returning to 2022 levels as mortgage rates drop over time. In fact, the group is set to open 30 more outlets in H2, translating to greater capacity to build more homes.

Building Its Way Up the Ladder

That being said, the main reason why Bellway is able to achieve such a rapid rate of growth as compared to some of its other peers like Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODY) (OTCPK:TWODF) and Persimmon (OTCPK:PSMMY) (OTCPK:PSMMF), is because it's one of the main beneficiaries of the government's new housing targets. This is due to the fact that the Newcastle-based firm is one of the bigger builders of family homes.

For context, the new Labour government has promised to shake up the UK's housing planning system that has been ridden with thick layers of red tape over the years. These include fast tracking the process of approving urban brownfield sites, mandatory housing targets, and the reintroduction of 5-year land supply rules, amongst many others.

While these rules have yet to take full effect, Bellway has, nonetheless, been one of the few housebuilders to have seen a rampant spike in its order book. This is partly because of the expansion of its sales network through its strong outlet opening programme, with financial initiatives such as cashbacks and a more competitive ASP resonating well with buyers.

Another positive for Bellway is the fact that a large portion of its landbank has received detailed planning consent. This means that it has a significant advantage in terms of future development potential, as it reduces the time and uncertainty associated that many of its other competitors have when it comes to building new homes and bringing them to market.

Combine the above with the fact that build activity has been ramping up, and one could make an argument that Bellway has got a sizeable tailwind blowing in its favour. This is reflected in the tick up in brick deliveries since the Labour government was elected, as it's often a leading indicator for completions. This should spell good news that the constructor is likely to hit its FY25 guidance.

Clearing the Way

The stock was previously trading at 22 times its forward earnings late last year, with much of the growth looking to have been priced in. However, the recent drop does look to have presented a lucrative buying opportunity, especially if the underlying thesis and guidance don't change over the medium term, with the stock now trading at a mere 12 times its forward earnings.

That is, of course, easier said than done given the volatile tariff dynamic at play. There's now a very real risk that a global trade war could unfold and trigger a recession into motion. After all, bank stocks, which are often a leading indicator for the housing market, have been falling from grace as investors alike begin to price in lower borrowing as a result of a recession.

The flip side, however, is that gilt yields have also fallen from their highs to touch YTD lows. Gilt yields are often a leading indicator for mortgage rates. So, if yields can fall further, we imagine lenders will follow suit with rate cuts. And if these do end up becoming big enough, they could stimulate the housing market further, and offset any supposed impact from tariffs.

And although there have been some downward revisions from Chancellor Rachel Reeves's housing targets, as she now only forecasts a gradual increase to achieve a build rate of 305k houses/yr by the end of parliament in 2029, it's still an improvement from the rates seen over the past few years. As such, we have conviction that Bellway would stand to benefit from this still.

But as we don't cover the stock, we refrain from issuing an official rating. That said, our model suggests upside of c.40.1%, with a forward dividend yield of c.2.8%. This is derived by comparing Bellway's key ratios to their historical and sector(s) averages, before averaging their resulting fair values altogether. Although, we stress that this is contingent on consensus estimates.

Price Model Bellway Sector AVG 5Y AVG Fair Value
EV/Underlying EBITDA (10% wt) 11.2 10.0 7.8 1,812p
P/E (10% wt) 15.8 15.3 14.5 2,139p
FP/E (25% wt) 12.0 16.7 10.8 2,595p
PEG (55% wt) 0.7 1.5 1.0 3,916p
Fair Value 3,200p

***As a reminder to our readers, Bellway's primary listing is on the London Stock Exchange (LSE:BWY). Trading this stock on OTCMKTS may present liquidity risks resulting in higher bid-ask spreads. For optimal execution and more accurate price discovery, we recommend assessing the stock on the LSE instead, where possible.

Explaining Our Approach and Model

When it comes to our valuation model, we employ a combination of multiples approach, rather than the infamous Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model, which many investment banks utilise. To explain, our methodology centres on utilising multiple valuation ratios with an emphasis on forward-looking earnings multiples:

  • EV/EBITDA (10% wt);
  • P/E (10% wt);
  • Forward P/E (25% wt);
  • (Forward) PEG (55% wt);

We prioritise forward earnings metrics because stock prices predominantly reflect future earnings expectations rather than past performance. Although, we include trailing ratios with lower weightings in order to provide analytical balance. This is to mitigate the effects of volatility or potential M&A activity.

We believe this approach delivers greater accuracy than a DCF, for the reason that it can account for critical factors such as stock buybacks more accurately. Furthermore, DCFs typically produce significantly higher standard deviations, as minor adjustments to the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (OTC:WACC) and/or discount rates can dramatically alter price targets.

In addition to that, DCF models often contain "black box" elements with inadequate explanations regarding the discount rate, WACC determinations, and more crucially, terminal value calculations from perpetual growth rates. On the other hand, our combination of multiples model is more transparent, and more digestible for investors.

Multiples Appendix

  1. EV/EBITDA (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1 '25 results.
    • Market cap data for EV is calculated from multiplying shares outstanding as of latest RNS with the latest share price.
    • Underlying EBITDA is calculated from: EBIT + Depreciation.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes positive EBITDA firms.
  2. P/E (10% wt)
    • As of TTM to H1 '25 results.
    • EPS is calculated from underlying diluted EPS on profit after tax, divided by diluted shares outstanding as of latest earnings RNS.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector, and only includes money-making firms.
  3. FP/E (25% wt)
    • Usually calculated from using weighted forward projections, if current period falls between fiscal years.
    • For example, if the current TTM is to H1 '25, the calculation will be: (FY25 projected EPS - H1 '25 EPS) + (FY26 projected EPS/100 x historical weight of H1 EPS on FY EPS).
    • EPS is calculated from diluted EPS on FTM profit after tax, divided by projected diluted shares outstanding.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by Refinitiv.
  4. FPEG (55% wt)
    • First, CAGR is calculated as: (FY27 EPS/TTM EPS) ^ (1/2.5 yrs) - 1.
    • Then, FPEG is calculated as: (Current share price/TTM EPS)/CAGR.
    • Sector average is the average of NYU Stern's EU Homebuilding sector.
    • EPS consensus data is provided by Refinitiv.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
